content:
Well-crafted puzzle! I would, however, classify it as “Easy” rather than “Medium”: given the current constraints (3 ≤ W, H ≤ 20), this corresponds to a grid of at most 400 cells. That’s tiny. Any algorithm, even a non-optimized recursive DFS, will pass the tests in a fraction of a millisecond. With grids this small, this puzzle is simple enough for anyone familiar with the “flood fill” principle. 
To keep the difficulty at “medium,” you could make the grid toroidal, for example. If you exit at the left edge, you reappear at the right edge. If you exit at the top, you reappear at the bottom :)

This puzzle has a similar style to https://www.codingame.com/training/hard/surface, but it's made slightly easier by only having to count the number of holes instead of the coordinates of each hole. It's a nice puzzle nonetheless!

As @5DN1L mentioned, please also add more irregularly-shaped holes, e.g.:

10 9
..........
.#########
#######..#
.###.#..#.
.#.#.....#
.#....##.#
.#.###.#.#
.#.#.#...#
.###.#####

Goal
* Consider rewriting this section for clearer logical flow and to reduce duplication:

You are given a 2D grid where {{#}} represents solid material and {{.}} represents empty space.

Empty space is connected using <<4-connectivity>> (up, down, left, right; diagonals do not count).

A <<hole>> is a connected region of {{.}} that does not touch the border of the grid.

Your task is to count the number of holes in the grid.

Output
* Please change "figure" to "grid".

Constraints
* Please change 100 to 20 or, alternatively, add cases to cover the range of 20 to 100 (though the largest grid would be smaller than 100 x 100 due to the input character limit).

Cases
* Please add a pair of cases where [[W]] < [[H]], and the grid should contain at least one filled # block (similar to Test 3) and holes that are more irregularly shaped.

Cover picture
* Please consider uploading a cover picture for the puzzle.

Thanks.
I hope the update will do :)

Easy puzzles should generally focus on fundamental concepts such as string manipulation, loops, and conditional logic. Algorithms like BFS and DFS typically fall into the Medium difficulty range. (Even though some existing puzzles that use them are currently labelled as Easy…)

Given that, I'd suggest keeping the difficulty as Medium.
Well, I guess that's quite subjective. I'm ok with that. I would have liked the grid to be open, but of course that's up to the author. thumbs up then ! :)

Thanks for the feedback! If you liked the topic and want harder versions, I could make ones in the near future ;) I'm a mathematician so it won't be hard to present some typical challenges of algebraic topology as games!

Thank you for your quick update.

Goal
* "The area outside the grid is considered empty (.). Any . cell on the border of the grid is therefore connected to the outside.": This part is no longer necessary because of the revised definition of a hole. We need not refer to connectivity with the outside.

Cases
* Tall grid with irregular hole (test): Please add two more #s to the top hole (the output becomes 2):

#####
#   #
# # #
### # <- add here
# # # <- add here
#####

* Tall grid with two irregular holes (validator): Please add three more #s to the bottom hole (the output becomes 4):

######
#  # # <- add here
# #### <- add here
###  # <- add here
#    #
######

Thanks again.
Thanks to you for your valuable feedback!
